[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後円墳]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "四隅突出型墳丘墓}}" to "四隅突出型墳丘墓|1==Protruded-corner square mounds}}"
revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]

Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "==Protruded-corner square mounds" to "=Protruded-corner square mounds"
revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=Protruded-corner square mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Protruded-corner square mounds" to "X-shaped Burial Mounds"

Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.

'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

[[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:if these are dates then move them to completion tier 5]]

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Square burial mounds]] to [[Category:Square Kofun]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.

=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square burial mound, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are burial mounds with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mounds}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group burial mounds" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner burial mounds already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />

revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Kofuns}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Kofun}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Attempting to force efresh]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Kofun}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]]

The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).

=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />

=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}

[[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Square Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] }} [[sn:Square Kofun]] [[ja:方墳]] [[sn:quare Kofun]] [[en:Square Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Square Kofun}}

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓|12=draft}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound|12=draft}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|12=draft}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)|12=draft}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳|12=draft}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=draft}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳|12=draft}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群|12=draft}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚|12=draft}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳|12=draft}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳|12=draft}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|12=draft}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳|12=draft}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=draft}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳|12=draft}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群|12=draft}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚|12=draft}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳|12=draft}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳|12=draft}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|12=draft}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>

'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==

[[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Square Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese archaeology]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Square Kofun]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Square Kofun]] [[ja:方墳]] [[sn:quare Kofun]] [[en:Square Kofun]] {{citation needed|Square Kofun}} [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it]] {{Kofun navbar}}

[[File:Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|thumb|right|250px|An example of a square Kofun, {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} (Sakae, Inba District, Chiba Prefecture)]]
'''Hōfun''' (方墳) are Kofuns with a square-shaped tumulus. They were constructed throughout the [[Kofun period]], second in number only to {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
The square mounds built in the early to mid-[[Kofun period]] are considered an extension of the [[Yayoi period]]'s {{ill|Kinki-style square-ditch tombs|ja|方形周溝墓|12=draft}} and the {{ill|San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound|12=draft}}. It is believed that their function and form underwent little change during this time. Recently, however, with the emergence of {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} and {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器|1=Haji pottery|12=draft}}, square mounds are increasingly regarded as a distinct type, leading to terms such as "early group Kofuns" (前期群集墳). The San'in-style projecting-corner Kofuns already incorporated diverse techniques found in the Kofun period, showcasing the advanced technology of ancient Izumo.<ref>''Introduction to Kofun for Beginners,'' Hirose Kazuo (ed.), pp. 72–73.</ref>
A prominent early example is the {{ill|Ōnari Kofun|ja|造山古墳 (安来市)|12=draft}} in Yasugi, Shimane Prefecture, which measures 60 meters on each side, making it the largest square mound in Japan. Square mounds are particularly concentrated in Izumo, with early examples of {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} also present. While generally small, square mounds were adopted for the tombs of powerful figures in the 7th century, when the construction of zenpō-kōen-fun ceased.
Examples include the {{ill|Kasuga Mukoyama Kofun|ja|春日向山古墳|1=Kasugayama Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Yōmei), measuring 63m x 60m, and the {{ill|Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|ja|山田高塚古墳|1=Yamada Takatsuka Kofun|12=draft}} (designated as the tomb of Emperor Suiko), measuring 63m x 56m. The {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|ja|石舞台古墳|12=draft}}, thought to be the tomb of {{ill|Soga no Umako|ja|蘇我馬子|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba Prefecture, with a side length of 80m and a height of 13m, are also noteworthy. The {{ill|Akasaka Tennozan Kofun|ja|赤坂天王山古墳|12=draft}} in Sakurai, measuring 45.5m x 42.2m with a height of 9.1m, has been linked to Emperor Sushun by scholar {{ill|Kōichi Mori|ja|森浩一|12=draft}}.
The largest square mound in terms of size is {{ill|Masuyama Kofun|ja|桝山古墳|12=draft}} in Nara Prefecture, followed by Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun in Chiba Prefecture.
== Regional Distribution == === Tōhoku Region === *{{ill|Iinozaka Kofun Cluster|ja|飯野坂古墳群|12=draft}} (Natori, Miyagi Prefecture): Includes two square mounds (観音堂北一号墳 and 二号墳).
=== Kantō Region === * The {{ill|Ryukakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳|12=draft}} in Chiba is the largest square mound of the terminal Kofun period.
=== Chūbu and Tōkai Regions === * The {{ill|Kawai Kofun Cluster|ja|河合古墳群|12=draft}} in Gifu includes the 30m-square mound {{ill|Jirōbei-zuka|ja|次郎兵衛塚|12=draft}}.
=== Chūgoku Region === * The {{ill|Kamihara Shrine Kofun|ja|神原神社古墳|12=draft}} in Shimane, an early square mound, measures 30m per side.
=== Kyūshū Region === * {{ill|Gionyama Kofun|ja|祇園山古墳|12=draft}} in Fukuoka is 23m per side, dating to the mid-3rd century, and is hypothesized as {{ill|Himiko|ja|卑弥呼|12=draft}}'s tomb.<ref>{{Cite book|author=Ishiyama Isao et al.|year=1979|title=Report on Gionyama Kofun Survey}}</ref>
== References == <references />
